result.markdown:
--- title: "Linear model" subtitle: "Fit voxel-wise first-level models through the typed fmrimod seam." jupyter: python3 execute: freeze: auto --- ## A Typed Spec and a Dataset The canonical first-level path is `fmri_lm(spec, dataset)`. The spec is a typed value built from `hrf(...)`; the dataset binds data to timing and events. No string formula, no hand-built design matrix. ::: {#51703773 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import fmrimod as fm from fmrimod.spec import hrf from fmrimod.contrast import condition rng = np.random.default_rng(5) events = pd.DataFrame({ "onset": np.arange(0, 96, 8), "condition": ["face", "scene", "tool"] * 4, "duration": 2.0, }) dataset = fm.fmri_dataset(rng.normal(size=(120, 6)), tr=1.0, events=events) spec = hrf("condition", basis="spmg1") ``` ::: ## Fit the First-Level Model `fmri_lm()` compiles the spec, builds the full event-plus-baseline design, and returns an inspectable result. Coefficients, standard errors, and *t*-statistics are typed accessors with matching shapes. ::: {#77468279 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} fit = fm.fmri_lm(spec, dataset) fit.betas.shape, fit.se().shape, fit.tstat().shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` ((4, 6), (4, 6), (4, 6)) ``` ::: ::: The realized design is inspectable from the fit — you never built it by hand, but you can still read every column. ::: {#9dba6c6c .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} pd.DataFrame( fit.betas, index=list(fit.model.design_matrix(run=0).columns), ).round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
0 1 2 3 4 5
condition_condition.face -0.055 0.013 0.013 -0.044 -0.021 0.049
condition_condition.scene -0.008 -0.026 -0.032 -0.018 -0.019 -0.013
condition_condition.tool -0.041 0.015 0.004 -0.006 -0.044 0.064
base_constant1_block_1 0.162 -0.049 0.130 0.222 0.222 -0.387
``` ::: ::: ## A Typed Contrast The hypothesis names conditions, not column indices. `fit.contrast(...)` returns a typed `ContrastResult`. ::: {#73f69331 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} face_vs_scene = fit.contrast( condition("face", term="condition") - condition("scene", term="condition"), name="face_vs_scene", ) face_vs_scene.stat_type, face_vs_scene.estimate.round(3), face_vs_scene.stat.round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` ('t', array([-0.047, 0.04 , 0.045, -0.027, -0.002, 0.062]), array([-1.668, 1.229, 1.795, -0.953, -0.085, 2.328])) ``` ::: ::: ## Matrix Helper for Numerical Canaries `fmri_ols_fit()` solves a design/data pair directly. It returns a plain dict and bypasses the typed seam — keep it for tests and benchmarks that deliberately want a low-level numerical canary, not for user-facing analyses. ::: {#4f38b919 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} X = np.asarray(fit.model.design_matrix_array(run=0)) matrix_fit = fm.fmri_ols_fit(dataset.get_all_data(), X) sorted(matrix_fit.keys()), matrix_fit["beta"].shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` (['XtXinv', 'beta', 'df', 'se', 't'], (4, 6)) ``` ::: ::: ## Next - [Contrasts](contrasts.qmd) - [Sketched and AR-aware models](sketched-ar-models.qmd)
